title: What is a map
instruction:
Think about a physical dictionary.

You look up the word "ephemeral" and it gives you the definition. You look up "serendipity" and it gives you that definition. You do not search by page number — you search by word.

A map works the same way. You look up a key and get its value back.

Run this. Then try looking up a different country.
successMessage: Look up by key, get the value. That is the entire concept of a map.

title: Declare a map with make
instruction:
When you want an empty map to fill later, use `make`.

`make(map[string]int)` creates an empty map where keys are strings and values are integers.

The key type goes first, value type second — both inside the brackets.

Add two scores to the map and print both.

codeChecks.message: Keep the make(map[string]int) declaration.
Add Alex's score: scores["Alex"] = 90
Add Sam's score: scores["Sam"] = 85
Print the map: fmt.Println(scores)
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: make(map[keyType]valueType) — empty map, ready to fill. Same make you used for slices.

title: Declare with values
instruction:
When you already have the data, declare and fill in one shot.

```
scores := map[string]int{
    "Alex": 90,
    "Sam":  85,
}
```

Note the comma after the last value — Go requires it when each entry is on its own line.

Add two more people to the map literal and print it.
hint: Add two more entries inside the curly braces, each on its own line with a comma at the end.

codeChecks.message: Add Jordan with a score: "Jordan": 88,
Add Casey with a score: "Casey": 76,
Replace the TODO comment with your two entries.

successMessage: Map literal syntax. Key: value, — the comma at the end of each line is required in Go when closing brace is on its own line.

title: Add and update a key
instruction:
Adding a new key and updating an existing key use the exact same syntax.

`scores["Jordan"] = 78` — if Jordan does not exist, it is added. If Jordan already exists, the value is replaced.

Go does not distinguish between insert and update. It is always just assignment.

Add a new person, then update an existing score, then print the map.

codeChecks.message: Add River: scores["River"] = 92
Update Alex: scores["Alex"] = 95
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: Same line adds and updates. If the key exists, the value changes. If not, the key is created. One syntax, two behaviours.

title: Read a value
instruction:
Reading from a map returns the value — or the zero value if the key does not exist.

For `map[string]int`, a missing key returns `0`. For `map[string]string`, it returns `""`.

No error. No crash. Just silently zero.

Run this. Notice what prints for a key that does not exist.
successMessage: Jordan is not in the map — Go returned 0 instead of crashing. That is convenient but also dangerous: you cannot tell if the key was missing or if the real value happened to be 0. That is what the next step fixes.

title: Check if a key exists
instruction:
The two-value assignment solves the ambiguity from the last step.

`val, ok := scores["Jordan"]`

- `val` is the value (zero if key missing)
- `ok` is `true` if the key exists, `false` if not

This is called the ok idiom. You will see it constantly in Go.

Add a check for a key that exists and one that does not.
hint: val, ok := scores["Alex"]  then fmt.Println(val, ok). Repeat for a missing key.

codeChecks.message: Use the two-value form: val, ok := scores["Alex"]
Also check for a missing key: scores["Jordan"]
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: val, ok — the ok idiom. It is Go's standard pattern for safe lookups. You will use this for maps, type assertions, channel reads, and more.

title: Delete a key
instruction:
`delete(map, key)` removes a key-value pair entirely.

If the key does not exist, nothing happens — no error.

Delete two people from the scores map and print the result.

codeChecks.message: Delete Sam: delete(scores, "Sam")
Delete Jordan: delete(scores, "Jordan")
Replace the TODO comment with your delete calls.

successMessage: delete() takes the map and the key. Two entries gone. The remaining two are unaffected.

title: len on a map
instruction:
`len(map)` returns how many key-value pairs are in the map.

Add two entries, delete one, and print the length after each operation.

codeChecks.message: Add oranges: inventory["oranges"] = 8
Delete bananas: delete(inventory, "bananas")
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: len works on maps just like slices. It tells you how many entries are currently stored.

title: Loop over a map
instruction:
`for key, val := range myMap` iterates over every key-value pair.

Important: maps are unordered. Every time you loop, the keys may come out in a different order. Go does this intentionally — do not rely on the order.

Loop through the scores and print each one as `"Alex: 90"`.
hint: for name, score := range scores { fmt.Printf("%s: %d\n", name, score) }

codeChecks.message: Use range to loop: for name, score := range scores
Use fmt.Printf to format the output.
Replace the TODO comment with your loop.

successMessage: Looping works. But notice the output order may vary each run — maps have no guaranteed order. If you need sorted output, sort the keys first.

title: Map with slice values
instruction:
A map's value can be any type — including a slice.

`map[string][]string` maps a string key to a list of strings.

This is perfect for things like: one person mapped to their list of hobbies.

Add a third person with two hobbies, then loop and print all of them.

codeChecks.message: Add Casey: hobbies["Casey"] = []string{"...", "..."}
Replace the TODO comment with your assignment.

successMessage: map[string][]string — a map where each value is a slice. One key, many values. You will see this pattern a lot.

title: Nested maps
instruction:
A map can also hold other maps.

`map[string]map[string]int` — outer key maps to an inner map.

Useful for hierarchical data: country → city → population.

Add a third country with two cities, then print the population of one specific city.
hint: population["Brazil"] = map[string]int{"Sao Paulo": 12300000, "Rio": 6700000}  then fmt.Println(population["Brazil"]["Sao Paulo"])

codeChecks.message: Add Brazil: population["Brazil"] = map[string]int{"City": number, ...}
Access a Brazilian city: population["Brazil"]["CityName"]
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: Nested maps. Access with double brackets: map[outer][inner]. Useful for any hierarchical data.

title: Build a word counter
instruction:
Classic map use case: count how many times each word appears.

Loop through the words slice. For each word, add 1 to its count in the map. If the word is not in the map yet, Go returns 0 — so `counts[word] += 1` works perfectly on the first hit too.

Add the counting loop, then print the map.
hint: for _, word := range words { counts[word]++ }  then fmt.Println(counts)

codeChecks.message: Increment the count for each word: counts[word]++
Loop through the words slice: for _, word := range words
Print counts: fmt.Println(counts)
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: Word counter done. This exact pattern powers search indexing, analytics dashboards, log parsers, and autocomplete engines. Maps make it trivial.
